---
title: "The Network Problem"
date: 2025-07-05
sidebar: true
---


```{=html}
<script>document.body.classList.add('book-page');</script>
```

There comes a point in the evolution of every sufficiently large computing system when adding another
processor ceases to make the system proportionally faster.


Computer scientists have known this for decades.


Gene Amdahl formalized one aspect of it in 1967 through what would later become known as Amdahl's
Law: any computation containing a sequential component eventually reaches a point of diminishing
returns, regardless of how many processors are added. Distributed systems researchers encountered
the same phenomenon from another direction. Databases scaled until network coordination became
dominant. Supercomputers scaled until message passing overshadowed floating-point arithmetic. Even
Google's earliest distributed infrastructure revealed a similar lesson. Computation was rarely the scarce
resource. Coordination was.


Modern language models have finally arrived at the same destination.


The public imagination often pictures frontier AI training as an enormous collection of GPUs performing
matrix multiplications in perfect synchrony, each accelerator independently contributing its share of the
computation. It is an appealing image because it suggests that intelligence emerges simply by
multiplying hardware. Reality is considerably messier.


Imagine attempting to write a novel with five thousand authors.


Suppose every sentence requires contributions from specialists distributed across different continents.
One author writes the introduction, another edits the grammar, a third verifies historical accuracy, while
a fourth rewrites the dialogue. Each individual may work astonishingly quickly. Yet after every
paragraph, they must pause, exchange drafts, reconcile conflicting edits and wait until everyone has
reached the same point before continuing.


Eventually the writing itself ceases to dominate the schedule.


Communication does.


This is remarkably close to what occurs during frontier model training.


The transformer, elegant though it appears on paper, fragments naturally across thousands of
accelerators. Parameters are partitioned. Activations are partitioned. Experts are partitioned. Gradients
are partitioned. Every optimization designed to make the model larger simultaneously increases the
amount of information that must travel between devices. At sufficiently large scales, the GPUs become
less like isolated computers and more like citizens participating in an extraordinarily complicated
conversation.


The mathematics of deep learning never anticipated this.


Backpropagation assumes gradients appear where they are needed. Matrix multiplication assumes
operands are available when computation begins. Neither algorithm concerns itself with the
geographical location of data because, historically, that location scarcely mattered. On a single
processor, moving information across memory is measured in nanoseconds. Across multiple GPUs, it is
measured in microseconds. Across multiple machines connected through InfiniBand fabrics, it becomes
slower still. The numerical values remain small in human terms, but they accumulate relentlessly across
trillions of operations.


Latency compounds.


Bandwidth saturates.


Entire clusters begin waiting.


One might reasonably expect this problem to disappear as networking technology improves. After all,
NVIDIA's NVLink provides extraordinary bandwidth compared with conventional PCIe, while InfiniBand
networks represent some of the fastest communication infrastructure ever constructed. Yet this
expectation misunderstands an important asymmetry. GPU computation has improved at a rate that
networking has struggled to match. Every new accelerator generation dramatically increases floating-
point throughput, but no comparable revolution has occurred in the speed with which information
travels between machines.


The consequence is subtle.


Each new generation of GPUs spends proportionally more time waiting.


Not because the hardware is inefficient.


Because the rest of the system cannot feed it quickly enough.


This observation explains why scaling modern language models increasingly resembles designing a
metropolitan transportation network rather than constructing a faster engine. Congestion, not
horsepower, determines throughput.


The challenge becomes particularly severe in sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures.


Recall from the previous chapter that only a small subset of experts participates in processing any given
token. This dramatically reduces computation, but it introduces a new requirement. Tokens must now
travel to whichever GPU happens to host the selected expert. Once processed, they must return before
subsequent computation can continue.


The arithmetic has become sparse.


The communication has not.


Indeed, in poorly designed systems, sparse architectures can paradoxically perform worse than dense
models because the communication overhead required to dispatch tokens overwhelms the
computational savings obtained through sparsity. The architecture succeeds theoretically while failing
operationally.


This distinction separates academic demonstrations from production-scale systems.


Sparse computation is easy.


Sparse communication is hard.


The engineering challenge therefore shifted from inventing better experts to transporting tokens more
intelligently.


DeepSeek's engineering contributions are particularly revealing in this regard because they
acknowledge something many discussions of Mixture-of-Experts quietly ignore: routers do not merely
assign work; they shape network traffic. Every routing decision carries consequences extending far
beyond model quality. Tokens assigned to distant experts incur communication latency. Experts
receiving uneven workloads create synchronization delays. Poor routing choices propagate through the
entire distributed system like traffic congestion spreading across a city's road network.


Consequently, optimizing expert routing became inseparable from optimizing network utilization.


This realization produced a series of innovations that, while less visible than new model architectures,
arguably contributed just as much to the economics of frontier training.


One example is **DeepEP** , a communication framework designed specifically for expert parallelism.
Rather than treating communication as an unavoidable interruption to computation, DeepEP attempts
to overlap the two. While one portion of the GPU performs useful numerical work, another
simultaneously orchestrates the transfer of tokens between experts. The objective is not to reduce
communication itself—a physical impossibility—but to prevent communication from creating idle
hardware.


This distinction is easy to miss.


Suppose a package delivery service cannot reduce the distance between two cities. It may still improve
overall efficiency by ensuring drivers continue delivering other packages while long-distance shipments
remain in transit. DeepEP applies an analogous philosophy to distributed inference and training.
Communication becomes another task to schedule rather than an event requiring the entire system to
pause.


A similar philosophy underlies **DualPipe** , another innovation associated with DeepSeek's training
infrastructure. Traditional pipeline parallelism often suffers from what practitioners refer to as pipeline
bubbles —intervals during which expensive accelerators remain idle because they are waiting for data
from adjacent stages. DualPipe reorganizes execution so that forward and backward computations
proceed concurrently whenever possible, allowing communication to disappear beneath useful work.
Like an experienced orchestra conductor, the system attempts to ensure that no section remains silent
while another performs.


What is striking about these contributions is that they possess almost nothing in common with
traditional machine learning research.


They belong instead to the intellectual tradition of operating systems, compiler design and high-
performance computing.


The neural network itself changes very little.


Everything surrounding it changes dramatically.


One begins to appreciate why describing DeepSeek or Qwen merely as "AI models" misses something
important. They are not simply statistical objects. They are distributed software systems spanning
thousands of accelerators, multiple memory hierarchies, sophisticated communication protocols and
increasingly intelligent runtime schedulers. Their capabilities emerge as much from systems
engineering as from optimization theory.


This observation, more than any benchmark, explains why frontier AI has become extraordinarily
difficult to reproduce. Publishing model weights is one thing. Reproducing the invisible infrastructure
that keeps tens of thousands of GPUs simultaneously productive is quite another. Increasingly, the
competitive advantage lies not only in the learned parameters but in the engineering discipline that
allows those parameters to be trained efficiently in the first place.


There is an irony here.


The transformer was originally celebrated because attention allowed every token to communicate with
every other token.


Years later, the greatest engineering challenge turned out not to be communication within the model,
but communication between the machines running it .


The boundary between machine learning and distributed systems had quietly dissolved.


Yet even after eliminating wasted computation, compressing memory movement and hiding network
latency, one uncomfortable question remained.


Why perform calculations using thirty-two bits when far fewer might suffice?


The next frontier would not concern where information traveled.


It would concern **how precisely that information needed to exist at all.**
